worth_it_explanation: Access hygiene in a business portfolio is a security and continuity concern, not a performance one, so the payoff is avoided harm rather than a lift in results. Each unneeded admin, each former employee or agency still in the People list, and each admin without two-factor authentication is a path an attacker can take to the ad accounts, the datasets, and the spend. The review is quick. Meta Business Settings lists every person, their role, and the assets they touch in one place, and removing or downgrading access takes a few clicks. The work pays off the first time a credential is phished or a contract ends and the access was already scoped to what the role needed.
intro: Business Manager access governance covers who can reach a brand's advertising assets in Meta Business Settings and at what level. The People list grants roles that range from full control of the whole portfolio down to a single asset with one task. These checks confirm that access is scoped to what each role needs, that people who have left are removed, and that the two-factor authentication requirement is enforced so a single phished password cannot reach the ad accounts.

audit_checks.title: People hold full control of the portfolio where partial, asset-scoped access would do
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Every person with full control can change billing, add or remove other people, and reach every ad account and dataset in the portfolio. A phished or shared credential at that level exposes the whole business, where a partial, asset-scoped role would have limited the blast radius to one asset and one task.

educate.base:
Meta Business Settings grants access in two layers. A person is first added to the business portfolio with a business-level role, then assigned to individual assets such as an ad account, a Page, or a dataset with a task-level permission on each. The business-level role of full control lets a person manage everything in the portfolio, including billing, the People list itself, and every asset. Partial access, by contrast, limits a person to the specific assets and tasks they are assigned.

The risk in granting full control by default is the size of the exposure if that account is compromised. A phished password on a full-control account reaches the ad accounts, the datasets, the Conversions API setup, and the ability to add new people, all at once. The same password on a partial-access account assigned to one ad account reaches that account and nothing else.

The principle of least privilege applies here. A person assigned to manage a single ad account's campaigns does not need full control of the portfolio, and an agency contractor working on one Page does not need access to billing or to the People list. Scoping each role to the assets and tasks the work requires keeps the access map matched to the actual responsibilities.

A few signs point to access that is broader than the work requires:
  • People with full control who only ever work in one ad account or on one Page
  • Contractors or agency staff granted business-level admin access rather than access to the specific assets they manage
  • Roles assigned once at onboarding and never narrowed as the person's responsibilities changed
You may want to review each person's role against what they actually do and consider downgrading anyone whose access is wider than their work. Meta lets you change a person's permissions without removing and re-adding them, so narrowing a role does not interrupt their access to the assets they keep.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the dataset that receives Conversions API (CAPI) and pixel events carries customer purchase signals, so a full-control role exposes that dataset alongside the ad accounts. You may want to confirm that people who only manage shopping campaigns are scoped to the relevant ad account and catalog rather than holding full control of the portfolio and its datasets.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the assets include Lead Ads forms and the dataset that stores submitted contact details, so a full-control role reaches that personal data. You may want to confirm that people who only manage lead campaigns are assigned to the specific ad account and form assets rather than the whole portfolio.

learn_more.source_title: About Business Portfolio and Business Asset Permissions
Change a Person's Permissions for a Business Portfolio or Business Asset

investigate.intro: List every person in the portfolio, note their business-level role, and compare it against the assets and tasks their work actually requires.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings, then under 'Users' select 'People' to see everyone added to the portfolio.
For each person, note whether they hold full control or partial access, and open their profile to see which ad accounts, Pages, and datasets they are assigned and at what task level.

Compare each role against the person's actual work. Flag anyone with full control who only operates within a single asset, and any contractor or agency member with business-level admin access rather than asset-level access.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per person, their business-level role, the assets they are assigned, and whether full control matches the scope of their work. A person whose access is scoped to the assets and tasks they use passes. A person holding full control while working within a single asset, or a contractor holding business-level admin access, is a fail recorded with the person's name and the narrower role that would fit.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each person's role and assigned assets above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several people in {{site_name}}'s Meta business portfolio hold full control where partial, asset-scoped access would cover their work. Full control reaches billing, the People list, and every ad account and dataset, so a single compromised credential at that level exposes the whole portfolio. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each person's role against the assets they actually use and downgrading anyone whose access is wider than their work, assigning contractors and single-asset operators to the specific ad accounts or Pages they manage rather than to the portfolio as a whole.

audit_checks.title: Former employees or agencies are still listed with access to the portfolio
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A person or agency that has left but remains in the People list keeps live access to ad accounts, datasets, and spend with credentials the business no longer controls. The risk runs from honest mistakes by someone who should no longer be working in the account to deliberate misuse after a contract ends badly.

educate.base:
Access in Meta Business Settings persists until someone removes it. When an employee leaves or an agency engagement ends, their entry in the portfolio's People list stays in place unless an admin takes the step to remove it. Until then, the person keeps whatever role and asset assignments they held, reachable through credentials the business no longer manages.

The exposure here is direct. A former agency that still appears under 'Partners' can continue to open ad accounts, edit campaigns, and read the datasets it was assigned. A former employee with a personal Facebook login still in the People list can do the same. Neither is under the business's control any longer, so the access is a standing path into the spend and the customer data with no oversight attached.

Two separate lists need a look. The 'People' list holds individuals added by personal Facebook account, which is where a former employee lingers. The 'Partners' list holds other businesses granted access, which is where a former agency lingers. A person can also be removed from one asset while remaining on another, so checking the portfolio level alone can miss residual asset-level access.

A few signs point to access that outlived the relationship:
  • Names in the People list that no longer correspond to current staff
  • Partner businesses listed under 'Partners' whose contract has ended
  • People removed from one ad account but still assigned to a Page or dataset
You may want to reconcile the People and Partners lists against a current roster of staff and active agreements, then remove anyone who no longer has a reason to be there. Removing a person revokes their access immediately, so the step closes the path the moment it is taken.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a former agency left under 'Partners' can still reach the catalog and the purchase dataset, which carry product and customer order data. You may want to confirm that any agency whose engagement has ended is removed from both the ad accounts and the catalog and dataset assets it was assigned.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a former employee or agency still in the lists can still reach Lead Ads forms and the contact details they collect. You may want to confirm that departed staff and ended agencies are removed from the form and dataset assets, not only from the ad account.

learn_more.source_title: Remove People From Your Business Portfolio

investigate.intro: Reconcile the People and Partners lists against current staff and active agreements, and remove anyone whose access has outlived their reason to have it.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings, then under 'Users' open 'People' and read every name against a current list of staff who should have access.
Open 'Partners' under 'Users' and read every listed business against the agencies and vendors that currently hold an active agreement.
For anyone in doubt, open their profile to see which ad accounts, Pages, and datasets they still reach since a person can be removed from one asset while remaining on another.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each person and partner in the lists and whether they correspond to current staff or an active agreement. A list that contains only current, accounted-for people and partners passes. Any name or partner business that no longer maps to a current relationship is a fail recorded with the name and the assets they still reach.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each person and partner against current staff and active agreements above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Meta business portfolio still lists former employees or agencies with live access. Access in Business Settings persists until someone removes it, so a departed person or an ended agency keeps whatever role they held, reachable through credentials the business no longer controls. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling both the People and Partners lists against current staff and active agreements, then removing anyone who no longer has a reason to be there and confirming the removal covers every asset they were assigned rather than the portfolio level alone.

audit_checks.title: The two-factor authentication requirement is not enforced for people in the portfolio
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without the enforced requirement, an admin can hold access to the ad accounts and spend behind a password alone. A phished or reused password then reaches the portfolio with nothing standing in the way, which is the common entry point for ad account takeover and fraudulent spend.

educate.base:
Two-factor authentication (2FA) adds a second step beyond the password when a person signs in from a device Meta does not recognize. Meta Business Settings lets an admin require it for everyone in the portfolio through the Security Center, with a choice between requiring it of admins only or of everyone with access. When the requirement is set, a person who has not turned on 2FA on their personal Facebook account cannot act in the portfolio until they do.

The reason the requirement matters is the nature of the credentials involved. Access to a Meta business portfolio runs through people's personal Facebook logins, which are frequent phishing targets and are often reused across other sites. A password alone is a single point of failure. When it leaks, the attacker reaches the ad accounts and the spend directly. The second factor blocks that path because the leaked password is no longer enough on its own.

Setting the requirement at the portfolio level is stronger than asking people to turn on 2FA themselves since a request can be ignored and an enforced requirement cannot. The Security Center reports who has 2FA on and who does not, which makes the gap visible before it is exploited rather than after.

A few signs point to a requirement that is not enforced:
  • The 2FA requirement in the Security Center is set to off or to no one
  • The Security Center lists admins or staff without 2FA turned on
  • The requirement was set to admins only while non-admin staff still reach the ad accounts
You may want to set the requirement to cover everyone with access rather than admins alone, then use the Security Center list to follow up with anyone who has not yet turned 2FA on.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, an ad account takeover can redirect budget to an attacker's campaigns and expose the purchase dataset, so the second factor protects both spend and customer data. You may want to confirm the requirement covers everyone who reaches the ad accounts, not admins alone.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a takeover can reach the contact details collected through Lead Ads forms, so the requirement guards personal data as well as spend. You may want to confirm 2FA is required of every person with access to the forms and their dataset.

learn_more.source_title: How to Turn on the Two-Factor Authentication Requirement in Your Business Portfolio

investigate.intro: Check the portfolio's two-factor authentication requirement setting and the Security Center list of who currently has it on.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings and go to the Security Center.
Read the two-factor authentication requirement setting and note whether it is off, set to admins only, or set to everyone with access.
Read the list of people and note anyone who shows as not having 2FA turned on, paying attention to non-admin staff if the requirement is scoped to admins only.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the requirement setting and, from the Security Center, which people have 2FA on. A requirement set to cover everyone with access, with no one showing as without 2FA, passes. A requirement set to off or to admins only, or a list showing people with access who have not turned 2FA on, is a fail recorded with the setting and the names without 2FA.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the requirement setting and the Security Center 2FA status for each person above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Meta business portfolio does not enforce the two-factor authentication requirement for everyone with access. Access runs through people's personal Facebook logins, so a password alone is a single point of failure, and a phished or reused password reaches the ad accounts and the spend with nothing in the way. {{voice_recommend}} setting the requirement in the Security Center to cover everyone with access rather than admins alone, then using the Security Center list to follow up with anyone who has not yet turned two-factor authentication on.
